reasoning: Sepsis is a clinical syndrome triggered by a dysregulated host response to a wide variety of pathogens (bacterial, viral, fungal), rather than a single specific infectious agent. Because of its massive etiological diversity, developing a universal "sepsis vaccine" is scientifically extremely difficult, if not purely impossible. While vaccines exist for specific pathogens that can lead to sepsis (such as pneumococcus), there is no unified vaccine for "sepsis" itself. The Wikipedia page "List of infectious diseases" correctly listed the Sepsis vaccine status as "No" on October 30, 2025. There are no late-stage clinical trials for anything resembling a universal sepsis vaccine that could be approved and added to Wikipedia between November 2025 and February 2026. Therefore, the probability of the status changing to "Yes" on Wikipedia is functionally zero, except for the marginal baseline risk of Wikipedia vandalism or an unusual categorical redefinition by Wikipedia editors. Assigning near the minimum allowed probabilities, slightly increasing over time to reflect the growing (but still marginal) risk of unexpected wiki edits over longer horizons.
